sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 3 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: MOUSE(row=38, col=10), DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 35, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: Green block got stuck at [38,10]-[39,11] after moving there. Arrow keys no longer move it. This might be because it's adjacent to the blue remnants at [34,8]-[37,11]. Need to investigate why it's stuck.
- Plan: Try clicking on the green block to reactivate it, or toggle the blue block to free up space. Also check the current board state.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 34 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
OK so now I see something important. The green block is at [40,12]-[41,13], NOT at [38,10]-[39,11] as I thought. Wait, but the output says:
"After click: board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True"
"After DOWN: level_completed=False"
"Green at: [40,12]-[41,13]"
So the click moved the green block from [38,10]-[39,11] to somewhere else, and then the DOWN and RIGHT didn't change it. But the output shows the green block at [40,12]-[41,13], which is the same position it was at before all those movements.
Wait, let me re-read the output. The segmentation was printed BEFORE the click, so it shows the state before the click. At that point, the green block was at [40,12]-[41,13]. But I thought it was at [38,10]-[39,11] based on the previous output.
Actually, looking more carefully at the previous output:
"After UP, LEFT: level_completed=False"
"Green at: [38,10]-[39,11]"
But then subsequent moves didn't change the position. So the green block was stuck at [38,10]-[39,11]. But now the segmentation shows it at [40,12]-[41,13].
Wait, I think I see the issue. The `seg` variable was assigned at the beginning of the code, before any actions. So when I printed the green block position after the click and DOWN/RIGHT, I was still using the old `seg` variable.
Let me re-check. The code does:
1. Assign `seg = current_frame.segmentation` (before any actions)
2. Print colored objects from `seg`
3. Click on green block
4. Try DOWN
5. Try RIGHT
6. Print green position from `seg` (still the old one!)
So the green block position printed at the end is from the OLD segmentation, not the updated one. That's why it shows [40,12]-[41,13] even though the block was at [38,10]-[39,11] before the click.
Actually wait, no. Looking at the initial print:
"color=N, size=4, bbox=[40,12]-[41,13]"
This was printed BEFORE the click. So the green block was already at [40,12]-[41,13] before the click. But the previous output said it was at [38,10]-[39,11].
Hmm, I'm confused. Let me re-read the previous output more carefully:
"After UP, LEFT: level_completed=False"
"Green at: [38,10]-[39,11]"
Wait, this was from the PREVIOUS python call. The green block was at [38,10]-[39,11] after the UP, LEFT action. Then subsequent LEFT, DOWN, RIGHT, UP actions didn't change it. So at the end of the previous python call, the green block was at [38,10]-[39,11].
But now, in the current python call, the segmentation shows the green block at [40,12]-[41,13]. This means something happened between the two python calls that moved the green block.
Actually, I think the issue is that the previous python call printed the green position using the OLD `seg` variable (assigned at the beginning), not the updated one. Let me re-read:
In the previous python call:
```python
seg = current_frame.segmentation  # This is the state BEFORE any actions in this call
# ... print statements using seg ...
actions = ['UP', 'LEFT']
result = action(actions)
# ... print using seg (still old!) ...
```
Wait no, looking at the previous code more carefully:
```python
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"  Green at: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")
```
This was inside a loop that re-assigned `seg` after each action. Wait, no it wasn't. Let me re-read the actual code...
Actually, looking at the previous code:
```python
# Try (38, 10)
result = action(['LEFT'])
print(f"After LEFT: level_completed={last_action_result.get('level_completed')}")
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        ...
```
The `seg` variable was NOT re-assigned after each action. It was only assigned once at the beginning. So all the printed positions were from the same old segmentation.
Wait, but the printed positions were all [38,10]-[39,11], which is consistent with the first action (UP, LEFT) moving the green block there. But if `seg` wasn't updated, then all the prints would show the SAME position, which is what happened.
So the green block was actually at [38,10]-[39,11] after the first action, and then the subsequent actions (LEFT, LEFT, LEFT, DOWN, DOWN, RIGHT, RIGHT, UP) didn't change it because it was stuck.
But now, in the current python call, the segmentation shows the green block at [40,12]-[41,13]. This means the click action (MOUSE at [38,10]) moved the green block from [38,10]-[39,11] to [40,12]-[41,13].
Wait, but the click was at [38,10], which is where the green block was. And the result was "board_changed=True, gameplay_changed=True". So the click did something.
Hmm, but the current segmentation shows the green block at [40,12]-[41,13], which is the same as the position BEFORE all the arrow key movements. So maybe the click "reset" the green block to its original position within the container?
Actually, I think the issue is that the green block was "stuck" because it was adjacent to the blue remnants. Clicking on it "freed" it and moved it to a different position.
OK so now I understand:
1. Green block was at [38,10]-[39,11] and stuck (couldn't move with arrow keys)
2. Clicking on it at [38,10] moved it to [40,12]-[41,13]
3. After that, DOWN and RIGHT didn't move it (it's at the bottom-right of the container)
Wait, but [40,12]-[41,13] is NOT at the bottom-right. The container is [38,8]-[43,13], so the bottom-right would be [42,12]-[43,13]. And [40,12]-[41,13] is in the middle-right.
Hmm, maybe the click moved the green block to a specific position, not just "freed" it. Let me think about this...
Actually, I think the click might have "picked up" the green block and moved it to a default position. Or maybe the click toggled some state.
Let me try a different approach. Let me click on different parts of the green block and see what happens. Also, let me try clicking on other objects to see if they do something.
Actually, let me step back and think about the puzzle differently. The current state shows:
- Blue block at [20,18]-[23,21] (original position)
- Red block at [24,18]-[29,21] (stamped position)
- Dark red block at [30,18]-[33,21]
- Blue remnants scattered at [34,8]-[37,11] area
- Green block at [40,12]-[41,13]
- Gray box with yellow at [18,22]-[23,27]
- Empty gray box at [30,8]-[33,11]
The blue remnants are scattered individual cells, not a coherent block. This suggests that when the blue block was stamped (moved to [34,8]-[37,11]), it left behind some "remnants" or the original position wasn't fully cleared.
Wait, actually looking at the blue remnants:
- [34,9]-[34,9] (1 cell)
- [34,11]-[34,11] (1 cell)
- [35,8]-[35,8] (1 cell)
- [35,10]-[35,10] (1 cell)
- [36,9]-[36,9] (1 cell)
- [36,11]-[36,11] (1 cell)
- [37,8]-[37,8] (1 cell)
- [37,10]-[37,10] (1 cell)
These are 8 individual blue cells in a checkerboard-like pattern. This looks like the "stamped" blue block that was partially erased or transformed.
Actually wait, the initial blue block was at [20,18]-[23,21] (16 cells, a 4x4 square). When stamped, it moved to [34,8]-[37,11] (also 4x4). But now the blue block is back at [20,18]-[23,21] (original), and there are scattered blue cells at the stamped position.
This suggests that when I toggled the blue block back to its original position, the stamped position wasn't fully cleared - some cells remained.
OK, I think I need to reconsider the puzzle mechanics. Let me think about what the goal might be:
1. Maybe the goal is to have the blocks arranged in a specific pattern
2. Maybe the goal is to "paint" or "color" certain areas
3. Maybe the goal is to match the right-panel shapes with the left-panel blocks
Let me try a completely different approach. Let me look at the right panel more carefully. The red shape and blue shape have white frames around them. Maybe clicking on the white frames does something different from clicking on the shapes themselves.
Actually, let me try clicking on the empty gray box at [30,8]-[33,11] to see if it does something. This box is empty and might be a target for something.
Also, I notice that the green block is inside a gray container. Maybe the green block needs to be moved to a specific position to "activate" something, like changing the color of another block or completing a pattern.
Let me try clicking on the empty gray box and see what happens.

sections.label: ASSISTANT
sections.content:
World model: Clicking the stuck green block at [38,10] moved it back to [40,12]-[41,13]. The green block seems to have special click behavior. Blue remnants are scattered cells at the stamped position. Need to understand the goal better.
Plan: Try clicking on the empty gray box and other interactive elements. Also try moving the green block to different positions systematically.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me first verify the current green position
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")

# Try moving green block with arrow keys from current position
# Current: [40,12]-[41,13]
# Try LEFT
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"After LEFT: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}], gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Try LEFT again
result = action(['LEFT'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"After LEFT: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}], gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Try UP
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"After UP: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}], gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Try UP again
result = action(['UP'])
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"After UP: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}], gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")

# Now try clicking on the empty gray box at [30,8]-[33,11]
result = action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 31, 'col': 10}])
print(f"\nClick empty gray box: board_changed={last_action_result.get('board_changed')}, gameplay_changed={last_action_result.get('gameplay_changed')}")
seg = current_frame.segmentation
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'N':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Green: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}]")

# Check if anything changed in the empty gray box area
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] == 'g':
        b = n['boundary']
        print(f"Gray box: [{min(p[0] for p in b)},{min(p[1] for p in b)}]-[{max(p[0] for p in b)},{max(p[1] for p in b)}], children={n['children']}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
